ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Bara Chirashi Don or Spicy Salmon Don combo; FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sushi — mixed fish and rice preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers sushi-type mixed fish and rice preparations; used as the closest defensible anchor for this chirashi-style rice bowl. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for bara chirashi don or spicy salmon don combo.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific Japanese rice bowl preparation.

main_ingredients: sushi rice (short-grain white rice, rice vinegar, sugar, salt)
spicy mayonnaise (mayonnaise, sriracha or chilli sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of approximately 450 g, comprising a split portion of bara chirashi don (assorted diced sashimi over seasoned sushi rice) and spicy salmon don (fresh salmon with spicy mayonnaise over seasoned sushi rice).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sushi, with fish (SR Legacy FDC ID 35188) and Salmon, Atlantic, raw (SR Legacy FDC ID 15076); composite estimation for mixed sashimi rice bowl
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers this exact half-and-half chirashi and spicy salmon don combination. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for sushi with fish (FDC ID 35188, ~150 kcal/100g, protein ~9.5g, fat ~5.8g, carbohydrate ~15g) and raw Atlantic salmon (FDC ID 15076), weighted to reflect the approximate 50/50 split of chirashi and spicy salmon components. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record (energy 151.11 kcal, protein 10.0g, fat 6.22g, carbohydrate 14.44g) are consistent with this USDA composite and are retained with medium confidence. Spicy mayonnaise contribution is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for mayonnaise and hot sauce components. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not contain a direct entry for bara chirashi don or spicy salmon don.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, sushi-type (short-grain, vinegared); Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) for short-grain white sushi rice (vinegared), which reports GI values in the range 72–80. The dominant carbohydrate source in this dish is seasoned sushi rice; sashimi and salmon components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 64.8) / 100 = 46.7, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 14.4 × 4.5 = 64.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 244 mg/100g (approximately 1,098 mg per 450g bowl), primarily from soy sauce and spicy mayonnaise, approaching one-half of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce, reduce or omit spicy mayonnaise, and limit pickled ginger to moderate the sodium load; the omega-3 fatty acids from salmon and assorted sashimi provide a beneficial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Seasoned short-grain sushi rice has a high glycemic index (GI ~72–80) and the 450g bowl delivers approximately 64.8g of carbohydrate with only 4.1g of dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic load (~47) that will cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced sushi rice portion or a cauliflower rice substitution, increase the proportion of sashimi and avocado relative to rice, and avoid or minimise spicy mayonnaise which contains added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.1g/100g; the dish provides beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon and assorted sashimi fish, with an estimated polyunsaturated fat content of 1.8g/100g supporting a favourable lipid profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat and high omega-3 content; sodium from soy sauce should be moderated by requesting low-sodium soy sauce, and spicy mayonnaise should be reduced to limit refined oil intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acid content (EPA/DHA) from salmon and assorted fatty fish directly reduces joint inflammation and suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines; saturated fat is very low at 1.1g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request low-sodium soy sauce to reduce sodium-related tissue swelling, retain wasabi and pickled ginger for their anti-inflammatory compounds, and minimise spicy mayonnaise to avoid pro-inflammatory refined oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Bara Chirashi Don Half Spicy Salmon Don is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish served in Singapore, combining two popular donburi preparations in a single bowl. The first half is Bara Chirashi Don, featuring assorted diced sashimi — typically salmon, tuna, and yellowtail — arranged over seasoned sushi rice (short-grain white rice seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt). The second half is Spicy Salmon Don, comprising fresh salmon sashimi dressed with spicy mayonnaise (a blend of Japanese mayonnaise and sriracha or chilli sauce) over the same seasoned sushi rice base. The dish is typically garnished with sesame seeds, nori (dried seaweed), pickled ginger, and wasabi, with soy sauce served on the side. The texture contrasts the soft, slightly sticky sushi rice with the silky, tender raw fish. The flavour profile is umami-rich from the sashimi and soy sauce, with a creamy, spicy kick from the mayonnaise component. Nutritionally, the dish provides 151 kcal per 100g, with 10g of protein and 6.2g of fat per 100g, making it a moderate-calorie, protein-rich meal. The omega-3 fatty acid content from salmon and assorted sashimi is clinically significant for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefit. However, the high-GI sushi rice base (GI ~72) and the 450g serving delivering approximately 64.8g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~47 make this dish a high glycemic load meal, requiring caution for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Sodium at 244 mg/100g (~1,098 mg per bowl) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. This dish is popular as a lunch or dinner option in Japanese casual dining restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved source covers this exact half-and-half bara chirashi don and spicy salmon don combination. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for sushi with fish (FDC ID 35188) and raw Atlantic salmon (FDC ID 15076), which are consistent with the input GrabFood-sourced values. The composite estimation approach introduces moderate uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients and the spicy mayonnaise contribution. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed literature for short-grain sushi rice as the dominant carbohydrate source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sushi with fish (FDC ID 35188) and raw Atlantic salmon (FDC ID 15076), weighted for the approximate 50/50 split of chirashi and spicy salmon components. Input GrabFood values are consistent with this USDA composite and are retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for salmon and mixed sashimi fish; these are not directly reported in the input record. 3. potassium_mg (220 mg/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for salmon and sushi rice components. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not directly available in USDA FoodData Central (consulted 2024-01-01) or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; individual component data exist in USDA SR Legacy but reliable composite values for this specific half-and-half preparation cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis of the assembled dish. 5. The ethnic_tag 'Alcoholic Drink' in the input record appears to be a data entry error and has been removed. 6. All core macros (protein_g 10.0g, fat_g 6.2g, carbohydrate_g 14.4g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a mixed sashimi and sushi rice bowl; no zero-macro justification is required. 7. GI of 72 is assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, AJCN) for short-grain vinegared sushi rice. Glycemic load per 450g serving = 46.7. 8. This dish is served in Singapore at Japanese casual dining restaurants; the primary cuisine is Japanese and the mandatory Japanese local-language name is provided. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases are also provided to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore context where this dish is commercially available.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name 'Half Bara Chirashi Don Half Spicy Salmon Don' is precise and unambiguous. Japanese local name provided as required by G6 for Japanese cuisine. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names also provided for Singapore market context. Romanized form also provided.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (151.0), protein_g (10.0), fat_g (6.2), carbohydrate_g (14.4) all present and numeric. Supported by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite adaptation (FDC IDs 35188 and 15076), documented in nutrient_source with match type 'adapted' and full explanation. No forbidden sources used.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.9), sodium_mg (244.0), saturated_fat_g (1.1), sugar_g (1.8) all present as numeric values, plausible and consistent with USDA SR Legacy sushi-with-fish data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (72) present; gi_category ('high') present; glycemic_load_per_serving (46.7) present; gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, AJCN) peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl dish) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FJP (sushi — mixed fish and rice preparation) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,650 characters), covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (high), Cardiovascular Disease (low). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included from input.

Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. No inconsistencies found.
